scenario: A FEEL expression needs coalesce behaviour -- return the first non-null value from a list of candidates. E.g., return customer.preferredEmail, else customer.primaryEmail, else "unknown@example.com". They wonder if FEEL has coalesce().
question: Does FEEL have a built-in coalesce function (first-non-null)?

options.text: md:70-91 -- returns value if non-null, otherwise default. primaryEmail). com")). Companion null-test built-in: is defined(value) per :29-59 (returns false for null or undefined-context-entry).

md or any other section of Camunda's FEEL built-in function catalogue. The canonical FEEL mechanism for null-fallback is get or else(value, default) for two candidates, or nested calls of that function for three or more candidates.

Use firstNonNull(a, b, c) to return the first non-null candidate value -- this function name is invented and has no entry in FEEL's built-in function library. Camunda's FEEL does not include a variadic first-non-null function under any name. md:70-91, which can be nested for three or more candidates or combined with list-filter projections.

FEEL has no mechanism for providing fallback values when a variable or expression evaluates to null -- this ignores the purpose-built get or else(value, default) function. md:70-91, get or else returns the first argument if non-null, otherwise returns the second argument. Combined with is defined() for undefined-path detection and list-filter projection patterns, FEEL provides comprehensive null-fallback capabilities without requiring any workaround.

a.text: Correct. Camunda FEEL solves null-fallback via the get or else built-in per feel-built-in-functions-boolean.md:70-91: Signature: ` get or else(value: Any, default: Any): Any Worked examples from the docs (:85-91): get or else("this", "default") // "this" get or else(null, "default") // "default" get or else(null, null) // null Two-candidate fallback (the common case): =get or else(customer.preferredEmail, customer.primaryEmail) Three-candidate fallback (nest): =get or else( customer.preferredEmail, get or else(customer.primaryEmail, "unknown@example.com") ) Multi-candidate via list + filter + first (canonical FEEL list filter with implicit item, per feel-list-expressions.md:67-86): =[customer.preferredEmail, customer.primaryEmail, "unknown@example.com"][item != null][1] The list filter list[predicate] uses the implicit item binding for each element; [1] takes the first match (FEEL is 1-indexed). NOT canonical FEEL (avoid -- anti-pattern): ❌ Bracket-comprehension [item in list : predicate] -- this DMN-spec syntax is fabricated for Camunda FEEL (Pattern #23). Camunda's filter syntax is list[predicate] with implicit item, not [binding in list : predicate]. ❌ Projection abuse: [v in [a, b, c] : v != null][1] mixes syntaxes that don't combine in Camunda FEEL. ❌ "Custom BKM with Boxed Function Expression" -- Boxed Function Expression body type is not in Camunda 8 modeler (Pattern #41); and BKMs are same-decision-only (Pattern #34) so they wouldn't share across decisions anyway. Companion null-checks: is defined(value) per :29-59 -- true if value is non-null and the context entry exists (handles null and undefined path access). instance of per feel-boolean-expressions.md:184-221` -- type narrowing within a guarded branch. Use cases: graceful defaults for optional fields, configuration cascades (instance-specific → project-default → hardcoded), display fallbacks (preferred → primary → "unknown").

b.text: Incorrect. No coalesce(...) in the FEEL boolean built-ins list -- get or else is the canonical two-arg fallback function.

d.text: Incorrect. get or else is purpose-built for this; list-filter projection covers multi-candidate cases.

explanation: Camunda FEEL solves null-fallback via the get or else built-in per feel-built-in-functions-boolean.md:70-91: Signature: ` get or else(value: Any, default: Any): Any Worked examples from the docs (:85-91): get or else("this", "default") // "this" get or else(null, "default") // "default" get or else(null, null) // null Two-candidate fallback (the common case): =get or else(customer.preferredEmail, customer.primaryEmail) Three-candidate fallback (nest): =get or else( customer.preferredEmail, get or else(customer.primaryEmail, "unknown@example.com") ) Multi-candidate via list + filter + first (canonical FEEL list filter with implicit item, per feel-list-expressions.md:67-86): =[customer.preferredEmail, customer.primaryEmail, "unknown@example.com"][item != null][1] The list filter list[predicate] uses the implicit item binding for each element; [1] takes the first match (FEEL is 1-indexed). NOT canonical FEEL (avoid -- anti-pattern): ❌ Bracket-comprehension [item in list : predicate] -- this DMN-spec syntax is fabricated for Camunda FEEL (Pattern #23). Camunda's filter syntax is list[predicate] with implicit item, not [binding in list : predicate]. ❌ Projection abuse: [v in [a, b, c] : v != null][1] mixes syntaxes that don't combine in Camunda FEEL. ❌ "Custom BKM with Boxed Function Expression" -- Boxed Function Expression body type is not in Camunda 8 modeler (Pattern #41); and BKMs are same-decision-only (Pattern #34) so they wouldn't share across decisions anyway. Companion null-checks: is defined(value) per :29-59 -- true if value is non-null and the context entry exists (handles null and undefined path access). instance of per feel-boolean-expressions.md:184-221` -- type narrowing within a guarded branch.
